quote: In Chinese hospitals, patients' data is strictly governed. No doctors or nurses are allowed to export sensitive data outside the hospital.
implication: Patient data must remain within the hospital network; on-premise hosting and export controls should be requirements.

quote: For imported machines and bots, the database should be based locally.
implication: Data containment: AI inference and storage must be co-located with the clinical site.

quote: Doctors will be held responsible for any mistakes made by medical bots, as they must review outputs before using them.
implication: Require explicit clinician sign-off and audit logs before AI outputs reach the patient.

quote: We don't really care about the running logic behind AI bots, doctors don't have time to dig deeper.
implication: Explanations must be lightweight and decision-relevant; full model explainability is a low priority for clinicians.

quote: You cannot take any output from these tools verbatim, you always need to sanity check, validate and verify.
implication: Require mandatory verification gates and human-in-the-loop checks around LLM outputs.

quote: The large model tried to 'fill in the gaps' and ended up generating fabricated data...increased the complexity of our manual review process.
implication: Detect and flag fabricated content; provenance tracking on every AI-generated artefact.

quote: Supposing there are 2 or 3 big AI models that are used across the country, that company has to bear the malpractice debt for whole country... I can see malpractice lawyers building class action lawsuits for all the people whose cancer was missed by the AI.
implication: Clarify liability allocation between vendor, deploying institution, and clinician before deployment; record decisions for audit.

quote: If an ML tool is 90% accurate, physicians will say it's clearly not ready. Yet they'll leave out that maybe physicians are only right 70% of the time... Physicians will tell you they've never made a mistake, and patients will tell you otherwise.
implication: Document and disclose AI accuracy alongside the human baseline used for comparison; avoid asymmetric evaluation.

quote: Let's say that an MRI scanner manufactured by GE is used in a memory clinic while one manufactured by Siemens is used in an ICU. So an algorithm would associate differences in manufacturers with neurodegenerative qualities and the presence of injury.
implication: Audit for site/equipment-driven shortcuts; stratify validation across scanner brands and clinical sites.

quote: Image files contain hidden patient identifiers (DICOM headers, burned-in pixel text).
implication: Strip metadata and run pixel-level de-identification before any image leaves the clinical environment.

quote: Clinicians won't trust a black-box prediction, but they don't want a 50-line SHAP explanation either — they want a one-line 'why'.
implication: Provide concise, decision-relevant rationales rather than raw feature attributions.

quote: Without versioned model snapshots and input logs we literally cannot reproduce why a patient got that score six months later.
implication: Mandate versioned model artefacts and append-only request/response logs for every inference.
